title: Refactor CI to use parent orchestrator workflow with reusable workflow_call children
Distributed rate limiting (ported from coding-tool)
description: Port rate limiting from coding-tool to eval. 11 categories (auth, join, execute, practice, trace, analyze, analyzeDaily, analyzeGlobal, sessionCreate, write, read) backed by Redis (Cloud Memorystore in prod, docker-compose redis locally) with in-memory fallback per-request when Redis is unavailable. Covers both go-backend API and executor service. See history/PLAN-rate-limiting.md for full design.

title: Core rate limit package (pkg/ratelimit)
description:
Create a new shared Go module at pkg/ratelimit/ that provides distributed rate limiting backed by Redis with in-memory fallback.

## Files to create

- pkg/ratelimit/go.mod — new Go module (github.com/jdelfino/eval/pkg/ratelimit), depends on github.com/redis/go-redis/v9
- pkg/ratelimit/limiter.go — Limiter interface, Result struct, Category config type
- pkg/ratelimit/category.go — all 11 category definitions (see below)
- pkg/ratelimit/redis.go — RedisLimiter using Lua scripts
- pkg/ratelimit/memory.go — MemoryLimiter (generalized in-memory sliding/fixed window)
- pkg/ratelimit/fallback.go — FallbackLimiter (try Redis, fall back to Memory on error, log warning)
- pkg/ratelimit/*_test.go — unit tests for each implementation

## Interface

type Limiter interface {
    Allow(ctx context.Context, category string, key string) (*Result, error)
}

type Result struct {
    Allowed   bool
    Remaining int
    ResetAt   time.Time
}

type Category struct {
    Name      string
    Algorithm string // "sliding" or "fixed"
    Limit     int
    Window    time.Duration
}

## Categories (matching coding-tool)

| Category | Algorithm | Limit | Window |
|---|---|---|---|
| auth | fixed | 5 | 1 min |
| join | sliding | 10 | 1 min |
| execute | sliding | 30 | 1 min |
| practice | sliding | 15 | 1 min |
| trace | sliding | 10 | 1 min |
| analyze | sliding | 5 | 1 min |
| analyzeDaily | fixed | 100 | 24 hr |
| analyzeGlobal | fixed | 750 | 24 hr |
| sessionCreate | sliding | 10 | 1 hr |
| write | sliding | 30 | 1 min |
| read | sliding | 100 | 1 min |

## Redis algorithms

- Sliding window: ZSET-based. Key = rl:{category}:{key}. Members = unique request IDs (or timestamps), scores = Unix timestamps. ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to evict expired, ZCARD to count, ZADD to record. All in a single Lua script for atomicity. Set TTL = window duration on the key.
- Fixed window: INCR + EXPIRE. Key = rl:{category}:{key}:{window_start}. INCR the key, EXPIRE with window duration on first increment. Simple and efficient.

## FallbackLimiter pattern

type FallbackLimiter struct {
    primary  Limiter // RedisLimiter
    fallback Limiter // MemoryLimiter
    logger   *slog.Logger
}

func (f *FallbackLimiter) Allow(ctx, category, key) (*Result, error) {
    result, err := f.primary.Allow(ctx, category, key)
    if err != nil {
        f.logger.Warn("redis rate limit failed, using in-memory fallback", "error", err)
        return f.fallback.Allow(ctx, category, key)
    }
    return result, nil
}

Note: The middleware layer must handle the case where even the fallback returns an error (nil result). In practice MemoryLimiter should never error, but the middleware should check for nil result to avoid panics.

## MemoryLimiter

Generalize the existing PracticeLimiter pattern from go-backend/internal/handler/practice.go:18-63. Use sync.Mutex + map[string][]time.Time for sliding window. For fixed window, use map[string]*fixedBucket with count + window start time. Key includes category prefix for namespace isolation.

**Periodic cleanup:** Add a background goroutine (started via Start()/Stop() lifecycle methods) that sweeps expired entries every 5 minutes. This prevents unbounded memory growth from users who stop making requests, especially important for 24-hour window categories (analyzeDaily, analyzeGlobal). The lazy cleanup on Allow() is retained as well for immediate reclamation.

## Testing

- MemoryLimiter: test sliding window accuracy (allow up to limit, reject at limit+1, allow after window expires)
- MemoryLimiter: test fixed window behavior
- MemoryLimiter: test cleanup goroutine removes stale entries
- FallbackLimiter: test Redis error triggers fallback, test Redis recovery resumes primary
- RedisLimiter: test with real Redis if available (integration test with build tag or skip)

title: Go backend rate limiting middleware and wiring
description:
Add rate limiting to all Go backend API routes using Chi middleware, backed by pkg/ratelimit.

## Files to create

- go-backend/internal/middleware/ratelimit.go — Chi middleware factory + key extraction helpers
- go-backend/internal/middleware/ratelimit_test.go — unit tests

## Files to modify

- go-backend/go.mod — add pkg/ratelimit and github.com/redis/go-redis/v9 dependencies
- go-backend/internal/config/config.go — no changes needed (RedisHost/RedisPort already exist)
- go-backend/internal/server/server.go — create Redis client, create FallbackLimiter, apply rate limit middleware to routes
- go-backend/internal/handler/session_analyze.go — add Limiter field to AnalyzeHandler, update NewAnalyzeHandler() to accept limiter, add in-handler daily limit checks (analyzeDaily + analyzeGlobal)
- go-backend/internal/handler/practice.go — remove PracticeLimiter struct (lines 18-63) and in-handler rate limit check (lines 117-121)
- go-backend/internal/handler/execute.go — remove practiceLimiter field from ExecuteHandler, remove NewPracticeLimiter call
- go-backend/internal/handler/practice_test.go — update tests (rate limiting now handled by middleware)
- go-backend/internal/handler/session_analyze_test.go — add tests for daily limit checks

## Middleware design

ForCategory returns a chi.Middleware for a given rate limit category:

```go
func ForCategory(limiter ratelimit.Limiter, cat string, keyFn KeyFunc) func(http.Handler) http.Handler

type KeyFunc func(r *http.Request) string

func UserKey(r *http.Request) string {
    user := auth.UserFromContext(r.Context())
    if user == nil { return "" }  // skip rate limiting if no user
    return user.ID.String()
}

func IPKey(r *http.Request) string {
    return r.RemoteAddr  // Already set by chi's RealIP middleware
}
```

429 response writes JSON body {"error": "rate limit exceeded"} with headers:
- X-RateLimit-Remaining: result.Remaining
- Retry-After: seconds until result.ResetAt

Must handle nil Result defensively (if both primary and fallback limiter fail, allow the request and log an error rather than panicking).

## Route wiring in server.go — IMPORTANT structural notes

The current route registration uses two patterns:
1. **r.Mount() with handler.Routes()** — most resource handlers (sessions, classes, sections, etc.) define their own sub-routers via Routes() methods. Rate limit middleware must be applied INSIDE the handler's Routes() method, not in server.go.
2. **Flat r.Post() calls in server.go** — execute, trace, analyze, practice, student routes (lines 245-283) are registered as flat calls. These can use r.With() directly.

For r.Mount()-based handlers, the approach is to pass the rate limit middleware as a parameter to the handler constructor or Routes() method. For example:

```go
// Option A: Pass rate limit middleware to handler
sessionHandler := handler.NewSessionHandler(rl)
// In SessionHandler.Routes():
r.With(readRL).Get("/", h.List)
r.With(sessionCreateRL).Post("/", h.Create)
```

For flat routes in server.go:
```go
r.With(executeRL).Post("/sessions/{id}/execute", ...)
r.With(practiceRL).Post("/sessions/{id}/practice", ...)
r.With(traceRL).Post("/sessions/{id}/trace", ...)
r.With(analyzeRL).Post("/sessions/{id}/analyze", ...)
```

For auth registration routes (mounted via r.Mount("/auth", authHandler.RegistrationRoutes(...))):
- Inject authRL into RegistrationRoutes method, or
- Refactor to apply r.With(authRL) before the mount

## Complete route-to-category mapping

| Category | Key | Routes |
|---|---|---|
| auth | IP | POST /auth/accept-invite, POST /auth/register-student, POST /auth/bootstrap |
| join | IP | POST /sections/join, POST /sessions/{id}/join |
| execute | User | POST /sessions/{id}/execute, POST /execute (standalone) |
| practice | User | POST /sessions/{id}/practice |
| trace | User | POST /sessions/{id}/trace |
| analyze | User | POST /sessions/{id}/analyze (per-minute; daily checks in-handler) |
| sessionCreate | User | POST /sessions |
| write | User | All other POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE: /auth/me (PUT), classes CRUD, sections CRUD, problems CRUD, sessions PATCH/DELETE/reopen/update-problem/feature, revisions POST, invitations CRUD, admin mutations, namespace CRUD, section instructors, regenerate-code |
| read | User | All GET endpoints: /auth/me, /realtime/token, classes/sections/problems/sessions list+get, /sessions/{id}/state, /sessions/{id}/public-state, /sessions/{id}/details, /sessions/{id}/students, revisions GET, admin stats/audit, dashboard, namespace list/get/users/capacity, invitations list/get, /sections/my, /sections/{id}/members |
| (none) | — | Health/readiness/metrics endpoints: /ping, /healthz, /readyz, /metrics |

## Analyze daily limits (in-handler)

Add a Limiter field to AnalyzeHandler and inject it via NewAnalyzeHandler(). In the Analyze method, after the per-minute middleware has passed:

```go
// Check global daily limit first (prevents wasting per-user quota)
result, err := h.limiter.Allow(ctx, "analyzeGlobal", "global")
if err != nil { /* log and allow */ }
if result != nil && !result.Allowed {
    // 429 with "Global daily analysis limit reached. Please try again tomorrow."
    // Retry-After: 86400
}

// Check per-user daily limit
result, err = h.limiter.Allow(ctx, "analyzeDaily", userID)
if err != nil { /* log and allow */ }
if result != nil && !result.Allowed {
    // 429 with "Daily analysis limit reached (100 per day). Please try again tomorrow."
    // Retry-After: 86400
}
```

## Testing

- Middleware unit tests: mock Limiter, verify 429 response format with correct headers, verify key extraction
- Middleware: verify that when KeyFunc returns "" (no user), request passes through without rate limiting
- Middleware: verify nil Result handling (allow request, log error)
- Analyze handler: test daily limit rejection messages (both global and per-user)
- Analyze handler: test that global limit is checked before per-user limit

title: Executor rate limiting migration to Redis
description:
Replace the executor service's in-process token bucket rate limiter with Redis-based distributed rate limiting using pkg/ratelimit.

## Context

The executor currently uses golang.org/x/time/rate (token bucket) with per-instance in-memory state. With replicated executors, each instance has independent limits. This task migrates to the shared pkg/ratelimit package using Redis for distributed state.

## Files to modify

- executor/go.mod — add pkg/ratelimit dependency, add github.com/redis/go-redis/v9, can remove golang.org/x/time/rate if no longer used
- executor/internal/config/config.go — add RedisHost (string, env REDIS_HOST) and RedisPort (int, env REDIS_PORT, default 6379). Keep RateLimitRPS and RateLimitBurst for now as they may be useful for tuning, or replace with the category-based config from pkg/ratelimit.
- executor/internal/server/server.go — replace rate.NewLimiter + rateLimitMiddleware with pkg/ratelimit.FallbackLimiter. Create Redis client from config. Apply to /execute and /trace endpoints.
- executor/internal/server/server_test.go — update TestRateLimitMiddleware_Rejects, TestRateLimitDisabled_WhenRPSZero, TestRateLimitEnabled_IntegrationWithServer to use new limiter
- executor/internal/config/config_test.go — add tests for RedisHost/RedisPort defaults

## Implementation

In server.go, replace:
```go
// OLD
if cfg.RateLimitRPS > 0 {
    limiter := rate.NewLimiter(rate.Limit(cfg.RateLimitRPS), cfg.RateLimitBurst)
    executeHandler = rateLimitMiddleware(limiter, executeHandler)
}
```

With:
```go
// NEW
var rl ratelimit.Limiter
if cfg.RedisHost != "" {
    redisClient := redis.NewClient(&redis.Options{
        Addr: cfg.RedisHost + ":" + strconv.Itoa(cfg.RedisPort),
    })
    rl = ratelimit.NewFallbackLimiter(
        ratelimit.NewRedisLimiter(redisClient),
        ratelimit.NewMemoryLimiter(),
        logger,
    )
} else {
    rl = ratelimit.NewMemoryLimiter()
}
// Apply to /execute and /trace using a simple middleware wrapper
```

The executor uses the "execute" category (30/min) for both endpoints, with a global key (not per-user, since the executor doesn't have user context — the Go backend already enforces per-user limits).

## Cleanup

Remove the old rateLimitMiddleware function (server.go lines 171-180) after replacing it. Remove the golang.org/x/time/rate import if no longer needed anywhere.

## Docker Compose

Update docker-compose.yml executor service to include REDIS_HOST=redis environment variable so local dev uses the shared Redis container.

## Testing

- Unit tests: verify new limiter wiring with mock Redis
- Verify graceful fallback when Redis not configured (MemoryLimiter used)
- Verify /execute and /trace still return 429 when rate limited

title: Infrastructure: Add Redis config to production deployments
description:
Add REDIS_HOST and REDIS_PORT environment variables to both Go backend and executor service production deployments so distributed rate limiting works in prod.

## Context

The Redis instance already exists (Cloud Memorystore, used by Centrifugo). Both services need the connection details in their configmaps/env vars. Without this, both services will fall back to in-memory-only rate limiting in production, defeating the purpose of distributed rate limiting.

## Files to modify

- k8s/base/executor-configmap.yaml — add REDIS_HOST and REDIS_PORT entries
- infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/main.tf — add REDIS_HOST and REDIS_PORT to the go-backend app-config ConfigMap (around lines 231-265). The Redis host is available from the redis module output (module.redis.host) or use the K8s service address (redis.default.svc.cluster.local).
- docker-compose.yml — add REDIS_HOST=redis to the executor service environment section (go-backend likely already has access via its existing config, but verify)

## Values

- REDIS_HOST: redis.default.svc.cluster.local (K8s service address, same as Centrifugo uses — see infrastructure/terraform/environments/prod/main.tf line 345)
- REDIS_PORT: 6379

## Verification

After deployment, verify both services connect to Redis by checking logs for rate limit fallback warnings — if no warnings appear, Redis is working.

title: Add handler-level integration tests for multi-repo endpoints
Go API Auth Layer
description:
## Summary
Implement authentication middleware for JWT validation and RBAC authorization in the Go API.

## Scope
- Cognito JWT validation middleware
- JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) fetching and caching
- User context extraction from validated tokens
- Role-based access control (RBAC) middleware
- Permission checking utilities

## JWT Validation
- Fetch and cache Cognito JWKS (refresh on key rotation)
- Validate token signature, issuer, audience, expiry
- Extract claims: sub, email, cognito:groups, custom attributes

## User Context
```go
type User struct {
    ID          uuid.UUID
    Email       string
    NamespaceID uuid.UUID
    Role        Role  // admin, instructor, ta, student
    Groups      []string
}

func UserFromContext(ctx context.Context) *User
```

## RBAC Middleware
```go
// Require specific roles for route groups
r.Group(func(r chi.Router) {
    r.Use(RequireRole(RoleInstructor, RoleAdmin))
    r.Post("/classes", createClass)
})
```

## Deliverables
- `internal/auth/` package with JWT validation
- `internal/middleware/auth.go` - authentication middleware
- `internal/middleware/rbac.go` - authorization middleware
- Unit tests with mock tokens
- Integration tests against Cognito (staging environment)

## Dependencies
- Requires: Go API Project Scaffolding (PLAT-4zm)
- Requires: Cognito SAML Integration (PLAT-29h)

## References
- See `/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` for auth middleware design
- AWS Cognito JWT structure documentation
close_reason: Merged: feat: Go API Auth Layer (#6)

title: Fix incorrect PromQL metric label and DB pool metric names in monitoring dashboard
description:
## Summary
The Cloud Monitoring dashboard PromQL queries reference incorrect metric names/labels, causing panels to show no data.

## Files to modify
- `infrastructure/terraform/modules/monitoring/main.tf`

## Bugs

### 1. HTTP metric label: `status_code` should be `status`
The Go middleware at `pkg/httpmiddleware/metrics.go:26` defines labels as `[]string{"method", "path", "status"}`, but the dashboard queries use `status_code`.

**Lines 32, 56**: Change `status_code` → `status` in:
- `sum by (status_code) (rate(http_requests_total[5m]))` → `sum by (status) (rate(http_requests_total[5m]))`
- `sum(rate(http_requests_total{status_code=~"5.."}[5m]))` → `sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))`

### 2. DB pool metric: `db_pool_acquired_connections` should be `db_pool_connections{state="acquired"}`
The Go code at `go-backend/internal/metrics/dbpool.go:44` exports `db_pool_connections` with a `state` label, not separate metrics.

**Lines 160, 184**: Change:
- `db_pool_acquired_connections / db_pool_max_connections` → `db_pool_connections{state="acquired"} / db_pool_max_connections`
- `db_pool_acquired_connections` → `db_pool_connections{state="acquired"}`

Also check line 204 for `db_pool_acquire_count_total` and `db_pool_canceled_acquire_count_total` against actual metric names in dbpool.go.
close_reason: Fixed: corrected status_code→status label and db_pool_acquired_connections→db_pool_connections{state=acquired} in monitoring dashboard PromQL queries

title: E2E build failure on PR #57: standalone output missing .next/static directory
description:
## Summary
PR #57 (feature/type-problem-fields) consistently fails E2E tests with:
```
cp: cannot create directory 'frontend/.next/standalone/.next/static': No such file or directory
```

This happens after a successful Next.js build ("Compiled successfully", all static pages generated). The E2E setup script expects frontend/.next/standalone/.next/static to exist, but the standalone output directory is not being created.

## Reproduction
Failed on both pre-rebase and post-rebase CI runs. All other checks pass (lint, typecheck, test, contract-coverage, contract-tests).

## Analysis
PR #57 only changes TypeScript types (types/api.ts, types/session.ts) and removes unsafe casts — no config changes. The standalone build works on main and on other PRs (PR #60, PR #56). This may be a Next.js build issue triggered by something in the frontend type changes, or a flaky CI runner issue that happens to be consistent for this PR.

## CI Runs
- Pre-rebase: https://github.com/jdelfino/eval/actions/runs/22048964105
- Post-rebase: https://github.com/jdelfino/eval/actions/runs/22049964297

## Files to investigate
- frontend/next.config.ts — verify output: 'standalone' is set
- scripts/run-e2e-tests.sh — the cp command that fails
- frontend/src/types/api.ts — PR changes (unlikely to affect build)

## PR Reference
PR #57: https://github.com/jdelfino/eval/pull/57
close_reason: Fixed by PR #61 (parallelized E2E setup); PR #57 now passes E2E

title: Typed realtime event library and contract tests
description:
## Summary

Realtime (Centrifugo WebSocket) event payloads have no type safety or contract tests on the frontend. The backend defines 8 event types with Go structs in `go-backend/internal/realtime/events.go`, but the frontend accesses payloads as untyped `any` — reading `payload.user_id`, `payload.code`, etc. with no TypeScript interfaces or validation. This already caused a real bug (PLAT-wka8.6: frontend read `payload.problem_id` but backend sends `payload.problem`).

The REST API has a robust pipeline: typed domain clients (`lib/api/*.ts`) → wire types (`types/api.ts`) → contract tests (`__tests__/contract/`) → 100% coverage enforcement (`check-contract-coverage`). Realtime events have none of this.

## Goals
1. TypeScript interfaces for all 8 event payloads, matching Go JSON tags exactly
2. A typed event parsing library that hooks consume instead of raw `payload.foo` access
3. Contract tests that verify event wire shapes against the real backend
4. Coverage enforcement ensuring new events get tested

## Current state

**Backend event types** (go-backend/internal/realtime/events.go):
- `student_joined` → `{ user_id, display_name }`
- `student_code_updated` → `{ user_id, code, execution_settings? }`
- `session_ended` → `{ session_id, reason }`
- `session_replaced` → `{ newSessionId }` (NOTE: camelCase inconsistency)
- `featured_student_changed` → `{ user_id, code, execution_settings? }`
- `problem_updated` → `{ problem_id }`
- `session_started_in_section` → `{ session_id, problem }`
- `session_ended_in_section` → `{ session_id }`

**Frontend consumers** (all access payload as untyped `any`):
- `useRealtimeSession.ts` — handles 6 event types (lines 224-366)
- `useRealtimePublicView.ts` — handles 3 event types (lines 93-133)
- `useSectionEvents.ts` — handles 2 event types (lines 33-81)

**Existing contract test infra:**
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/validators.ts` — shape validators for REST responses
- `frontend/scripts/check-contract-coverage.ts` — 100% coverage enforcement for `lib/api/*.ts`
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/realtime.integration.test.ts` — tests REST endpoints only, NOT event payloads

## Known issues to fix along the way
- `SessionReplacedData.newSessionId` uses camelCase while all other fields are snake_case

title: Add TypeScript interfaces for realtime event payloads
Create typed realtime event parsing library
description:
## Summary
Create a typed event parsing module that hooks consume instead of accessing raw `payload.foo`. This mirrors how `lib/api/*.ts` wraps REST endpoints with typed functions. Hooks will import parse functions and get type-safe payloads.

## Files to create
- `frontend/src/lib/api/realtime-events.ts` — Typed event parsers

## Design

```typescript
import { RealtimeEventType, StudentJoinedData, ... } from '@/types/realtime-events';

// Type-safe event discriminator
// Hooks call: const parsed = parseRealtimeEvent(ctx.data)
// Then switch on parsed.type to get narrowed payload type
interface ParsedEvent<T extends RealtimeEventType, D> {
  type: T;
  data: D;
  timestamp: string;
}

type RealtimeEvent =
  | ParsedEvent<'student_joined', StudentJoinedData>
  | ParsedEvent<'student_code_updated', StudentCodeUpdatedData>
  | ParsedEvent<'session_ended', SessionEndedData>
  | ParsedEvent<'session_replaced', SessionReplacedData>
  | ParsedEvent<'featured_student_changed', FeaturedStudentChangedData>
  | ParsedEvent<'problem_updated', ProblemUpdatedData>
  | ParsedEvent<'session_started_in_section', SessionStartedInSectionData>
  | ParsedEvent<'session_ended_in_section', SessionEndedInSectionData>;

export function parseRealtimeEvent(raw: unknown): RealtimeEvent;
```

The function validates the envelope shape (has `type`, `data`, `timestamp`) and returns a discriminated union. Hooks switch on `.type` and TypeScript narrows `.data` automatically.

Note: The discriminated union is named `RealtimeEvent` (not `RealtimeEventEnvelope` — that's the raw wire type in types/realtime-events.ts from PLAT-pp4r.1).

## Files to modify
- `frontend/src/hooks/useRealtimeSession.ts` — Replace raw `payload.foo` with `parseRealtimeEvent()` + typed switch
- `frontend/src/hooks/useRealtimePublicView.ts` — Same
- `frontend/src/hooks/useSectionEvents.ts` — Same
- `frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/useRealtimeSession.test.ts` — Update mock payloads and event handling to match new parsed-event access pattern
- `frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/useSectionEvents.test.ts` — Same

## Reference files
- `frontend/src/lib/api/realtime.ts` — existing typed API client pattern
- `frontend/src/hooks/useRealtimeSession.ts:224-366` — current untyped event handling
- `frontend/src/hooks/useRealtimePublicView.ts:93-133` — current untyped event handling
- `frontend/src/hooks/useSectionEvents.ts:33-81` — current untyped event handling

## Implementation steps
1. Create `frontend/src/lib/api/realtime-events.ts` with `parseRealtimeEvent()`
2. Refactor `useRealtimeSession.ts` publication handler to use parsed events
3. Refactor `useRealtimePublicView.ts` publication handler
4. Refactor `useSectionEvents.ts` publication handler
5. Update `useRealtimeSession.test.ts` mock payloads and assertions for the new access pattern
6. Update `useSectionEvents.test.ts` mock payloads and assertions for the new access pattern
7. Verify all hook tests pass

## Verification
`make test-frontend && make lint-frontend && make typecheck-frontend`

title: Fix SessionReplacedData camelCase inconsistency
description:
## Summary
`SessionReplacedData` in `go-backend/internal/realtime/events.go:48-51` uses `json:"newSessionId"` (camelCase) while all other event payloads use snake_case (`user_id`, `display_name`, `session_id`, etc.). This is the only camelCase field across all 8 event types.

## Files to modify
- `go-backend/internal/realtime/events.go:50` — Change `json:"newSessionId"` to `json:"new_session_id"`
- `frontend/src/types/realtime-events.ts` — Change `newSessionId` to `new_session_id` in `SessionReplacedData` interface
- `frontend/src/lib/api/realtime-events.ts` — Update `SessionReplacedData` field reference in parser if needed
- `frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/useRealtimeSession.test.ts` — Update test payloads (mock events now use `new_session_id`)

Note: This task depends on PLAT-pp4r.2. After .2 lands, hooks no longer access `payload.newSessionId` directly — they use the parsed discriminated union. So the hook files themselves (`useRealtimeSession.ts`, etc.) do NOT need changes; only the type definition, parser, and test mocks need updating.

## Risk
This is a wire format change. If any client is caching old-format events (unlikely for ephemeral WebSocket messages) this could break. Centrifugo doesn't persist messages long-term, so this is safe to change. Both frontend and backend deploy together.

## Verification
`make test-api && make lint-api && make test-frontend && make lint-frontend && make typecheck-frontend`

title: Add contract tests for realtime event payload shapes
description:
## Summary
Add contract tests that verify Centrifugo event payload shapes match between Go backend and frontend TypeScript interfaces. Currently, contract tests only cover REST API endpoints — the event wire format has zero automated validation.

## Approach
The contract test should trigger backend actions that publish events, subscribe to the Centrifugo channel, and validate the received payload shape against the TypeScript interfaces.

## Separate test target — NOT part of existing contract tests
These tests require a running Centrifugo instance (WebSocket server + subscription token generation), which the existing contract tests (`make test-integration-contract`) do not. The infrastructure needs are fundamentally different:
- Existing contract tests: HTTP request/response against Go backend
- These tests: subscribe to Centrifugo WebSocket, trigger backend action via REST, collect async event, validate shape

This MUST be a separate test target (e.g., `make test-integration-realtime-contract`) with its own Jest project config. The target should:
- Require `CENTRIFUGO_URL`, `CENTRIFUGO_WS_URL`, `CENTRIFUGO_TOKEN_SECRET`, `CENTRIFUGO_API_KEY` env vars
- **Fail hard** if env vars are missing (not silently pass like some existing tests do with `console.warn` + `return`)
- Use the `centrifuge` npm package (centrifuge-js) in Node.js (testEnvironment: 'node')
- Generate JWT subscription tokens signed with CENTRIFUGO_TOKEN_SECRET

## Files to create
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/realtime-events.integration.test.ts` — Contract tests for event payloads (new Jest project, separate from existing contract tests)

## Files to modify
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/validators.ts` — Add shape validators for each event payload type
- `frontend/jest.config.ts` — Add new Jest project for realtime contract tests
- `Makefile` — Add `test-integration-realtime-contract` target

## Validators to add (following existing pattern in validators.ts)
- `validateStudentJoinedShape(obj)` — checks user_id (string), display_name (string)
- `validateStudentCodeUpdatedShape(obj)` — checks user_id, code, execution_settings (optional)
- `validateSessionEndedShape(obj)` — checks session_id, reason
- `validateSessionReplacedShape(obj)` — checks new_session_id (or newSessionId until .3 lands)
- `validateFeaturedStudentChangedShape(obj)` — checks user_id, code, execution_settings (optional)
- `validateProblemUpdatedShape(obj)` — checks problem_id
- `validateSessionStartedInSectionShape(obj)` — checks session_id, problem
- `validateSessionEndedInSectionShape(obj)` — checks session_id
- All validators must call `expectSnakeCaseKeys()` (except SessionReplaced if .3 hasn't landed)

## Test scenarios
For each event type, trigger the backend action via REST API and verify the Centrifugo event:
1. Create session → receive `session_started_in_section` on section channel
2. Student joins → receive `student_joined` on session channel
3. Student updates code → receive `student_code_updated` on session channel
4. Feature student → receive `featured_student_changed` on session channel
5. Update problem (PATCH) → receive `problem_updated` on session channel
6. End session → receive `session_ended` on session channel + `session_ended_in_section` on section channel
7. Delete session and create new → receive `session_replaced` on old session channel

## Reference files
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/validators.ts` — existing validator pattern
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/realtime.integration.test.ts` — existing realtime contract tests (REST only)
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/globalSetup.ts` — test setup (creates session, etc.)
- `frontend/src/lib/centrifugo.ts` — Centrifugo client setup
- `go-backend/internal/realtime/integration_test.go` — Go-side Centrifugo test patterns (subscribe + collect, token generation)

## Verification
`make test-integration-realtime-contract`

title: Add realtime event contract coverage enforcement
description:
## Summary
Extend the contract coverage enforcement script to also track realtime event types, ensuring every event type defined in `types/realtime-events.ts` has at least one contract test validating it.

## Files to modify
- `frontend/scripts/check-contract-coverage.ts` — Add scanning for realtime event type coverage in contract tests

## Design
The existing script scans `lib/api/*.ts` for exported async functions and checks they're imported in contract tests. Extend it with a two-step check for event types:

1. **Validator existence:** Scan `types/realtime-events.ts` for exported interfaces matching `*Data` pattern. For each, check that `validators.ts` exports a corresponding `validate*Shape()` function (e.g., `StudentJoinedData` → `validateStudentJoinedShape`).
2. **Test usage:** Scan contract test files for imports of those `validate*Shape` functions from `./validators`.
3. Report uncovered event types (missing validator OR unused validator) alongside uncovered API functions.

This two-step approach is needed because contract tests import validators (not the type interfaces directly). Checking for type imports would produce false negatives since the validators encapsulate the type knowledge.

## Reference files
- `frontend/scripts/check-contract-coverage.ts:42-146` — current scanning logic
- `frontend/src/types/realtime-events.ts` — event type interfaces (created in .1)
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/validators.ts` — validator functions (extended in .4)

## Verification
`make check-contract-coverage`

title: Log and surface email send failures on invitation create
description:
## Summary
The invitation Create handler silently discards email send errors (`_ = h.emailClient.SendInvitation(...)` at go-backend/internal/handler/invitations.go:127). This means invitations appear successful (HTTP 201) even when no email is sent, with no log trace.

In contrast, the Resend handler (go-backend/internal/handler/invitations.go:247-249) correctly returns HTTP 500 on email failure.

## Files to modify
- `go-backend/internal/handler/invitations.go` lines 125-127 (Create handler)
- `go-backend/internal/handler/invitations.go` lines 267-269 (SystemCreate handler, same issue)

## Implementation steps
1. Replace `_ =` with proper error handling: log the error at WARN/ERROR level
2. Do NOT fail the request — the invitation record is already created, so the user can retry via the resend endpoint
3. Consider adding a response field like `email_sent: bool` so the frontend can show a warning
4. Add the same fix to SystemCreate which has the identical pattern
close_reason: Covered by PLAT-2b6.3 (subtask of epic PLAT-2b6)

title: Fix ExecutionResult type: output and error should be optional (omitempty)
close_reason: Already fixed in commit e4f6c44 (PR #78): ExecutionResult output and error are optional in both Go (omitempty) and TypeScript (?) types

title: Handle orphaned Firebase accounts on backend registration failure
description:
## Summary
If `createUserWithEmailAndPassword` succeeds but the subsequent backend API call fails (500 error, network timeout, invitation consumed), the Firebase account exists but no user profile is created in the database. The user:
- Has a Firebase Auth account
- Has no profile in the backend database
- Gets 'auth/email-already-in-use' error if they retry

## Affected files
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/invite/accept/page.tsx:287-298`
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/register/student/page.tsx:250-261`

## Fix approach
Delete the Firebase account on backend failure using `deleteUser()` so the user can retry cleanly. Wrap the backend call in try/finally or catch block that calls `auth.currentUser?.delete()` on failure.

## Alternative approaches
- Sign in existing Firebase user before creating (but then need to handle partial state)
- Backend idempotent registration endpoint (more complex)

The simplest fix is to delete the Firebase account on backend failure.

title: Eliminate intra-file test dependency chain in admin contract tests
description:
## Summary

admin.integration.test.ts has a sequential dependency between updateSystemUser and deleteSystemUser tests. Both depend on tempUserId created in beforeAll, but deleteSystemUser also implicitly depends on updateSystemUser having changed the role first (though this is cosmetic — the real issue is that deleteSystemUser nullifies tempUserId, so test order matters).

## Files to modify

- frontend/src/__tests__/contract/admin.integration.test.ts

## Implementation

1. Give deleteSystemUser its own temp user created in a local beforeAll, separate from the one used by updateSystemUser
2. Each describe block should be independently runnable
3. Verify each describe block passes when run in isolation via `jest --testNamePattern`

## Current dependency

```
beforeAll → creates tempUserId (line 31, set at line 52)
  ↓ used by
updateSystemUser() (line 183) — changes role
deleteSystemUser() (line 198) — deletes user, sets tempUserId = null
```

title: AI-Assisted Problem Creation Engine
description:
Go library powering AI-assisted creation of programming assignments. Instructor provides course context (PDF slides/chapters), answers a short intake questionnaire (topic, size, theme, notes), and iterates with a conversation engine to produce complete, validated assignments.

Output: directory of files — README.md, starter code, solution, public tests (student self-check), hidden tests (grading/bug-hunting), problem.yaml config.

Key design decisions:
- Conversation engine (not tool provider) — library manages the full LLM interaction internally. Hosts just pipe user messages in and display responses out.
- LLMClient interface abstracts provider (Anthropic first, swap in OpenAI/Gemini later)
- Tests are the crown jewel: structural (compiles, right names), public (basic correctness), hidden (edge cases, boundary conditions)
- Validation by shelling out to pytest locally
- Python as first target language
- CLI host first, web integration later
- No platform DB or git integration yet — standalone output
- Default model: Sonnet (configurable). ~$2-3/assignment at 25 turns.

title: Module scaffolding + LLM client interface + Anthropic implementation
description:
Set up the Go module and package structure for the problemgen library. Create the LLMClient interface that abstracts the LLM provider, and implement the Anthropic provider.

Module path: github.com/jdelfino/eval/problemgen

Files to create:
- problemgen/go.mod — new Go module (github.com/jdelfino/eval/problemgen)
- problemgen/llm.go — LLMClient interface, ChatRequest, ChatResponse, ToolDef, ToolCall, Message, TokenUsage types
- problemgen/anthropic.go — Anthropic implementation of LLMClient using their API (check for official Go SDK; if none, use REST directly)
- problemgen/anthropic_test.go — unit tests with HTTP mock

LLMClient interface:
  Chat(ctx context.Context, req ChatRequest) (*ChatResponse, error)

ChatRequest: System string, Messages []Message, Tools []ToolDef
ChatResponse: Text string, ToolCalls []ToolCall, Usage TokenUsage

The Anthropic implementation handles:
- tool_use content block format
- Message role mapping
- API key auth (x-api-key header, NOT Bearer token)
- Model selection (configurable, default claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929)
- Error handling + retries for rate limits

Note: the existing go-backend/internal/ai/Client interface is a different abstraction (single-shot code analysis vs. multi-turn conversation with tool use). No need to consolidate — these serve different purposes.

title: Problem artifacts model + file writer
description:
Define the data model for generated problem artifacts and implement writing them to disk.

Files to create:
- problemgen/artifacts.go — ProblemArtifacts, ProblemSpec, ProblemConfig, ProblemIntake structs
- problemgen/writer.go — Write() function that produces directory structure on disk
- problemgen/writer_test.go — tests for file writing

ProblemArtifacts struct:
  Spec         ProblemSpec        // title, description, difficulty, topics, language
  StarterCode  map[string]string  // filename -> content (multi-file support)
  Solution     map[string]string  // filename -> content
  PublicTests  map[string]string  // tests students can see
  HiddenTests  map[string]string  // tests for grading/bug-hunting
  Config       ProblemConfig      // test_command, timeout_seconds

ProblemIntake struct:
  Topic  string  // e.g. 'recursive tree traversal with DFS'
  Size   string  // e.g. '3 hours', 'short in-class exercise'
  Theme  string  // optional, e.g. 'zombie apocalypse survival game'
  Notes  string  // optional, e.g. 'students have seen linked lists but not trees'

Output directory structure:
  <output-dir>/
    problem.yaml        # ProblemConfig serialized
    README.md           # ProblemSpec.Description
    starter/            # StarterCode files
    solutions/          # Solution files
    tests/public/       # PublicTests files
    tests/hidden/       # HiddenTests files

Write() creates the directory, writes all files, returns the output path. Should handle creating subdirectories, overwriting existing output cleanly.

title: Engine core — conversation loop with tool use
description:
Implement the core Engine struct that manages the LLM conversation, internal tool definitions, and tool call handling. This is the heart of the library.

Files to create:
- problemgen/engine.go — Engine struct, Start(), AddContext(), Chat(), Artifacts() methods
- problemgen/tools.go — internal tool definitions (update_spec, write_starter_code, write_solution, write_public_tests, write_hidden_tests, update_config, run_validation)
- problemgen/system_prompt.go — skeletal system prompt (task PLAT-qli.5 does the substantive prompt work)
- problemgen/engine_test.go — tests using a mock LLMClient

Engine struct:
  client    LLMClient
  messages  []Message           // conversation history
  artifacts *ProblemArtifacts   // current generated state
  config    EngineConfig

EngineConfig:
  OutputDir       string
  TargetLanguage  string  // 'python' initially
  ValidateOnGen   bool

Core conversation loop in Chat():
  1. Append user message to history
  2. Send full history + system prompt + tool definitions to LLMClient
  3. If response has tool calls:
     a. Execute each tool (update artifacts in memory)
     b. Append tool results to history
     c. Send again (loop back to 2)
  4. If response is text only, return Response to host

Start(ctx, intake) bootstraps the conversation:
  - Formats ProblemIntake into the first user message
  - Runs one Chat() cycle to get the engine's opening proposal

AddContext() — this task implements a text-only version:
  - Appends course material text as a user message
  - Runs one Chat() cycle so engine acknowledges what it understood
  - PLAT-qli.4 adds PDF extraction on top of this

Internal tools — each updates ProblemArtifacts:
  update_spec(title, description, difficulty, topics) -> updates Spec
  write_starter_code(filename, content) -> updates StarterCode map
  write_solution(filename, content) -> updates Solution map
  write_public_tests(filename, content) -> updates PublicTests map
  write_hidden_tests(filename, content) -> updates HiddenTests map
  update_config(test_command, timeout_seconds) -> updates Config
  run_validation() -> stub/no-op until PLAT-qli.6 integrates the real validator

Response struct includes:
  Text      string            // conversational text for host to display
  Artifacts *ProblemArtifacts // non-nil if artifacts changed this turn
  Status    EngineStatus      // gathering/generating/refining/validating/complete

Depends on: PLAT-qli.1 (LLMClient interface), PLAT-qli.2 (artifacts model)

title: PDF text extraction
description:
Add PDF text extraction to the engine's AddContext() method. Task PLAT-qli.3 creates a text-only AddContext() that appends raw text to the conversation. This task adds PDF support by extracting text from PDF bytes before passing to the existing AddContext() flow.

Files to create:
- problemgen/pdf.go — ExtractText(pdfBytes []byte) (string, error)
- problemgen/pdf_test.go — tests with sample PDFs

Files to modify:
- problemgen/engine.go — update AddContext() to handle CourseMaterial with Type='pdf' by calling ExtractText() before appending to conversation

CourseMaterial struct (add to engine.go or a new context.go):
  Type     string  // 'pdf', 'text' (extensible later)
  Name     string  // e.g. 'week5-recursion.pdf'
  Data     []byte  // raw file bytes (for PDF)
  Text     string  // pre-extracted text (for text type)

AddContext() changes:
  1. If Type='text': use Text field directly (already works from PLAT-qli.3)
  2. If Type='pdf': call ExtractText(Data) to get text, then follow same path
  3. Frame the text as a user message: 'Here are the course materials for [Name]. Use this as context for the assignment:\n\n[extracted text]'
  4. Append to conversation history
  5. Run one Chat() cycle so the engine acknowledges what it understood

PDF library evaluation needed — options:
  - pdfcpu: pure Go, good for structure but text extraction can be limited
  - unipdf: commercial license, full-featured
  - go-fitz (mupdf bindings): excellent extraction, requires CGO
  - ledongthuc/pdf: simple pure Go text extraction

Start with simplest option that extracts readable text from slide PDFs. Can upgrade later. Pure Go preferred to avoid CGO complexity.

title: Test generation — system prompt + hidden/public test tools
description:
The crown jewel. Engineer the system prompt and tool definitions for generating comprehensive, robust, focused test suites. Both public (student-visible) and hidden (grading) tests.

Files to modify:
- problemgen/system_prompt.go — add detailed test generation guidance to the system prompt
- problemgen/tools.go — refine write_public_tests and write_hidden_tests tool definitions

System prompt must encode expertise about:

1. Structural tests (both public and hidden):
   - Code compiles / imports correctly
   - Expected class/function names exist with correct signatures
   - Correct parameter types and return types
   - Proper module structure (for multi-file)
   - These catch the 'silly' mistakes every student makes

2. Public tests (student self-check during development):
   - Basic correctness — happy path with simple inputs
   - A few representative examples from the problem description
   - Clear, descriptive test names that hint at what's being tested
   - Should give students confidence their approach is right without giving away edge cases

3. Hidden tests (grading / bug-hunting):
   - Edge cases: empty input, single element, boundary values, overflow
   - Corner cases specific to the algorithm (e.g. unbalanced trees, duplicate keys)
   - Performance: large inputs to catch O(n^2) when O(n) is expected
   - Style deviations: global variables, hardcoded values, missing error handling
   - Common student mistakes: off-by-one, wrong base case, mutation of input

Test quality principles to encode:
   - Focused: one concept per test function
   - Descriptively named: test name explains the bug it would catch (e.g. test_empty_list_returns_none, not test_edge_case_1)
   - Robust: not brittle to valid alternative implementations (e.g. don't assert exact floating point, don't depend on dict ordering)
   - Independent: tests don't depend on each other's state
   - Deterministic: no randomness, no timing-dependent assertions

Target: pytest for Python. Test files should be runnable with 'pytest tests/ -v'.

Acceptance criteria:
  1. Generate 3 sample problems via the engine (beginner, intermediate, advanced):
     - Beginner: e.g. FizzBuzz or list reversal
     - Intermediate: e.g. binary search tree operations
     - Advanced: e.g. graph shortest path
  2. For each, verify the generated test suite includes:
     - At least 1 structural test (function/class exists with correct signature)
     - At least 3 public tests with descriptive names
     - At least 5 hidden tests covering edge cases
     - All test names describe the bug they catch (no test_1, test_2, etc.)
  3. Run validation: solution passes all tests
  4. Spot-check: manually introduce a common bug (e.g. off-by-one) and verify at least one hidden test catches it

This task is primarily about prompt engineering — the code changes are small but the prompt content is critical. Expect iteration.

title: Validation — run solution against tests
description:
Implement the Validator that runs the generated solution against all tests (public + hidden) and returns structured results. Feed results back into the conversation so the LLM can fix issues.

Files to create:
- problemgen/validator.go — Validator struct, Run() method
- problemgen/validator_test.go — tests with known-good and known-bad solutions

Validator.Run(ctx, artifacts) flow:
  1. Create temp directory
  2. Write solution files to temp dir
  3. Write all test files (public + hidden) to temp dir under tests/
  4. exec.CommandContext('pytest', 'tests/', '-v', '--tb=short')
  5. Parse stdout: extract per-test pass/fail and test names
  6. Capture stderr for error output
  7. Clean up temp dir
  8. Return ValidationResult

ValidationResult struct:
  Passed      bool
  TestResults []TestResult   // per-test: name, passed, output
  Stderr      string
  Duration    time.Duration

TestResult struct:
  Name    string  // e.g. 'test_traversal.py::test_empty_tree'
  Passed  bool
  Output  string  // captured output or failure message

Integration with Engine:
  - When the LLM calls the run_validation tool, Engine calls Validator.Run()
  - Results are formatted and returned as the tool result
  - LLM sees which tests pass/fail and can fix solution or tests
  - Engine.Validate() is also exposed directly for the host to call

Requirements: Python 3 + pytest must be installed. Return a clear error if not found.

Depends on: PLAT-qli.2 (artifacts model)

title: CLI host (REPL)
description:
Minimal CLI wrapping the Engine for instructor use. Collects intake questionnaire, runs conversation loop, handles /pdf and /write commands.

Files to create:
- problemgen/cmd/problemgen/main.go — CLI entry point

Configuration:
  ANTHROPIC_API_KEY environment variable (required). Error clearly if missing:
    'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not set. Get one at https://console.anthropic.com/'

CLI flow:
  1. Parse flags: --language (default: python), --model (default: sonnet), --output-dir (default: temp)
  2. Read ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from environment, exit with clear error if missing
  3. Create LLMClient (Anthropic) and Engine
  4. Prompt intake questionnaire (stdin):
     - Topic: (required)
     - Expected completion time: (required)
     - Theme (optional):
     - Other notes (optional):
  5. Call engine.Start(ctx, intake)
  6. Display engine's opening response
  7. Enter REPL loop:
     - Read user input from stdin
     - Handle special commands:
       /pdf <path> — read file, call engine.AddContext()
       /write — call engine.Write(), display output path
       /validate — call engine.Validate(), display results
       /artifacts — display current artifact summary
       /quit — exit
     - Otherwise: call engine.Chat(ctx, input), display response
  8. Display artifact changes inline when Response.Artifacts is non-nil

UX considerations:
  - Use golang.org/x/term for readline-style input if available
  - Show artifact diffs when files are updated (or at least list what changed)
  - Display token usage/cost after each turn (from Response via TokenUsage)
  - Handle Ctrl+C gracefully (offer to write current artifacts before exit)

Depends on: PLAT-qli.3 (engine core), PLAT-qli.4 (PDF extraction), PLAT-qli.6 (validation)

title: Makefile + CI integration

Add build, test, and lint targets for the problemgen module to the project Makefile and CI pipeline.

Files to modify:

New targets:

Also update CLAUDE.md quality gates table to include the new module.

Depends on: PLAT-qli.1 (module exists)

title: Add read-only database user for production debugging
description:
Add a read-only PostgreSQL user (`reader`) to the production Cloud SQL instance for safe debugging.

## Why
Currently the only DB user is `app` which has full CRUD. When debugging production issues, agents and operators should connect as a read-only user to prevent accidental mutations. The `/debug-prod` skill documents this user but it doesn't exist yet.

## Implementation
1. **Terraform** (`infrastructure/terraform/modules/cloudsql/main.tf`):
   - Add a `google_sql_user.reader` resource with a generated password
   - Add outputs for the reader password and connection string
2. **SQL migration** — grant read-only privileges:
   ```sql
   GRANT CONNECT ON DATABASE eval TO reader;
   GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA public TO reader;
   GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA public TO reader;
   ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES IN SCHEMA public GRANT SELECT ON TABLES TO reader;
   ```
3. **Kubernetes** — add `READER_DATABASE_PASSWORD` to `app-secrets` (or a separate secret)
4. **Update** `scripts/db-proxy.sh` usage docs to reference reader user
5. **Update** `.claude/skills/debug-prod/SKILL.md` to remove the 'does not exist yet' note
close_reason: Implemented reader user in Terraform + migration 006 + updated skill

title: Auto-publish problem to section when starting a session
Add EnsureSectionProblem store method
description:
## Summary

Add an idempotent store method that ensures a section_problems record exists for a given (section, problem) pair. Uses INSERT ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING so it's safe to call even when the problem is already published.

## Files to modify

- `go-backend/internal/store/section_problems.go` — add `EnsureSectionProblem()` method
- `go-backend/internal/store/interfaces.go` — add method to `SectionProblemRepository` interface

## Implementation steps

1. Add method signature to `SectionProblemRepository` interface in `interfaces.go`:
   ```go
   EnsureSectionProblem(ctx context.Context, params CreateSectionProblemParams) error
   ```

2. Implement in `section_problems.go`:
   ```go
   func (s *Store) EnsureSectionProblem(ctx context.Context, params CreateSectionProblemParams) error {
       query := `INSERT INTO section_problems (section_id, problem_id, published_by, show_solution)
           VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4)
           ON CONFLICT (section_id, problem_id) DO NOTHING`
       _, err := s.q.Exec(ctx, query, params.SectionID, params.ProblemID, params.PublishedBy, params.ShowSolution)
       if err != nil {
           if e := HandleForbidden(err); e != err {
               return e
           }
           return err
       }
       return nil
   }
   ```

3. Add integration test in `section_problems_integration_test.go` verifying:
   - Creates record when not exists
   - No-ops when record already exists (doesn't change show_solution)
   - Returns no error in either case

title: Auto-publish problem in session creation handler
description:
## Summary

Modify the session creation handler to auto-publish the problem to the section when creating a session. This ensures students can join sessions even if the instructor hasn't manually published the problem. Accepts an optional show_solution parameter.

## Files to modify

- `go-backend/internal/handler/sessions.go` — modify `Create` handler and `createSessionRequest`

## Implementation steps

1. Add `ShowSolution *bool` field to `createSessionRequest`:
   ```go
   type createSessionRequest struct {
       SectionID    uuid.UUID  `json:"section_id" validate:"required"`
       ProblemID    *uuid.UUID `json:"problem_id"`
       ShowSolution *bool      `json:"show_solution"`
   }
   ```

2. In `Create` handler, after fetching the problem (line ~157) and before creating the session (line ~170), add:
   ```go
   // Ensure problem is published to the section (idempotent).
   showSolution := false
   if req.ShowSolution != nil {
       showSolution = *req.ShowSolution
   }
   if err := repos.EnsureSectionProblem(r.Context(), store.CreateSectionProblemParams{
       SectionID:    req.SectionID,
       ProblemID:    *req.ProblemID,
       PublishedBy:  authUser.ID,
       ShowSolution: showSolution,
   }); err != nil {
       httputil.WriteInternalError(w, r, err, "internal error")
       return
   }
   ```

3. Add/update handler unit test verifying:
   - Session creation with problem_id calls EnsureSectionProblem
   - Blank session (no problem_id) does NOT call EnsureSectionProblem
   - show_solution defaults to false when not provided
   - show_solution=true is passed through

title: Update createSession frontend API and add publish UX to session modals
description:
## Summary

Update the frontend createSession API to support show_solution parameter. Add publish UX to both session creation modals showing a forced "Publish to section" indicator and a "Show solution" toggle.

## Files to modify

- `frontend/src/lib/api/sessions.ts` — add showSolution param to createSession()
- `frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/CreateSessionFromProblemModal.tsx` — add publish UX
- `frontend/src/app/(app)/instructor/components/StartSessionModal.tsx` — add publish UX

## Implementation steps

### API client (`sessions.ts`)
1. Add optional `showSolution?: boolean` parameter to `createSession()`:
   ```typescript
   export async function createSession(
     sectionId: string,
     problemId?: string,
     showSolution?: boolean
   ): Promise<Session> {
     const body: Record<string, unknown> = { section_id: sectionId };
     if (problemId) body.problem_id = problemId;
     if (showSolution !== undefined) body.show_solution = showSolution;
     return apiPost<Session>('/sessions', body);
   }
   ```

### CreateSessionFromProblemModal
1. Import `listProblemSections` from `@/lib/api/section-problems`
2. Add state: `publishedSectionIds` (Set<string>), `showSolution` (boolean, default false)
3. On mount (alongside loading sections), fetch `listProblemSections(problemId)` and build set of published section IDs
4. When section is selected, check if `publishedSectionIds.has(selectedSectionId)`:
   - If NOT published: show info box with forced "Publish to section" (checked, disabled) + "Show solution to students" toggle
   - If ALREADY published: show small note "Already published to this section"
5. Pass `showSolution` to `createSession(selectedSectionId, problem_id, showSolution)` when not already published

### StartSessionModal  
1. Import `listSectionProblems` from `@/lib/api/section-problems`
2. Add state: `publishedProblemIds` (Set<string>), `showSolution` (boolean, default false)
3. On mount, fetch `listSectionProblems(section_id)` (already have section_id as prop) and build set of published problem IDs. Pass a dummy user_id or use a dedicated endpoint — actually `listSectionProblems` requires userID in backend. Alternative: use `listProblemSections(problemId)` when a problem is selected.
4. When a problem is selected, check if it's in the published set:
   - Same UX as CreateSessionFromProblemModal
5. Pass `showSolution` to `createSession()`

### Tests
- Unit tests for both modals verifying publish UX shows/hides appropriately
- Unit test for createSession API client with showSolution parameter

title: Switch to social provider authentication (Google, GitHub, Microsoft)
description: Replace email/password auth with social provider sign-in (Google, GitHub, Microsoft) via Identity Platform. Eliminates passwords, email verification, and password reset. Access controlled via bootstrap env var, invitations, and join codes. Resend stays for invitation emails only.

title: Replace CLI bootstrap with BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_EMAIL env var and add email_verified checks
description:
## Summary
Replace the bootstrap CLI tool with a BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_EMAIL env var. The first user to sign in with a matching verified email (via any social provider) becomes system-admin. Also add email_verified checks to all registration endpoints to prevent email spoofing from providers that don't verify.

## Files to modify
- `go-backend/internal/config/config.go` — add `BootstrapAdminEmail` field
- `go-backend/internal/handler/auth.go` — update PostBootstrap, add email_verified checks
- `go-backend/internal/server/server.go` — pass BootstrapAdminEmail to AuthHandler (check all NewAuthHandler call sites)
- `go-backend/internal/store/interfaces.go` — add `SystemAdminExists` to UserRepository interface
- `go-backend/internal/store/users.go` — implement `SystemAdminExists`

## Files to delete
- `go-backend/cmd/bootstrap/` — entire directory (CLI tool no longer needed)
- Makefile targets: `build-bootstrap` (~line 40-41), `bootstrap` (~line 175-176)

## Implementation steps

### 1. Add config field (`config.go`)
Add to Config struct:
```go
BootstrapAdminEmail string `env:"BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_EMAIL"`
```
No default, no production validation (empty means bootstrap disabled).

### 2. Add SystemAdminExists store method
Add to `UserRepository` interface in `interfaces.go`:
```go
SystemAdminExists(ctx context.Context) (bool, error)
```
Implement in `users.go`:
```go
func (s *UserStore) SystemAdminExists(ctx context.Context) (bool, error) {
    var exists bool
    err := s.q.QueryRow(ctx, "SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM users WHERE role = 'system-admin')").Scan(&exists)
    return exists, err
}
```
Add stub in test repos that panics (matching existing `stubRepos` pattern).

### 3. Update AuthHandler (`auth.go`)
Add `bootstrapAdminEmail string` field to AuthHandler struct. Update constructor to accept it.

**Note:** `NewAuthHandler` may be called in multiple places in `server.go`. Check all call sites and update them all.

### 4. Add email_verified check
Add a check to `PostBootstrap`, `PostAcceptInvite`, and `PostRegisterStudent`:
```go
claims := auth.ClaimsFromContext(r.Context())
if !claims.EmailVerified {
    httputil.WriteError(w, http.StatusForbidden, "email address must be verified by your sign-in provider")
    return
}
```
Verify that the `Claims` struct in `go-backend/internal/auth/jwt.go` already has an `EmailVerified` field. If not, add it and parse from the JWT `email_verified` claim.

### 5. Update PostBootstrap handler
Replace custom claim check with:
```go
if h.bootstrapAdminEmail == "" || claims.Email != h.bootstrapAdminEmail {
    httputil.WriteError(w, http.StatusForbidden, "not authorized to bootstrap")
    return
}
if !claims.EmailVerified {
    httputil.WriteError(w, http.StatusForbidden, "email address must be verified by your sign-in provider")
    return
}
```
Keep the existing duplicate-detection pattern via `HandleDuplicate` on the `CreateUser` call for idempotency — if the same admin retries, the unique constraint on `external_id` catches it and returns 409. Do NOT add a separate `SystemAdminExists` check before the create — let the DB constraint handle retries naturally. Only use `SystemAdminExists` if needed for a pre-check UI hint (optional).

Actually on reflection: skip `SystemAdminExists` entirely. The current `HandleDuplicate` pattern on `external_id` is sufficient for idempotency. A second bootstrap by a different email would fail because `BootstrapAdminEmail` is a single email. A second bootstrap by the same email would hit the unique constraint. This keeps the code simpler.

### 6. Update server.go
Pass `cfg.BootstrapAdminEmail` to all `NewAuthHandler` call sites.

### 7. Delete CLI (`go-backend/cmd/bootstrap/`)
Delete entire directory. Remove Makefile targets `build-bootstrap` and `bootstrap`.

### 8. Update tests
- Update auth handler tests: test bootstrap with email match, email mismatch, empty config, duplicate (409)
- Test email_verified rejection on all three registration endpoints
- Update config tests: add BootstrapAdminEmail to test data
- Remove any bootstrap CLI tests
- Update mock repos if needed (no SystemAdminExists since we're skipping it)

### Verification
- `make test-api && make lint-api` passes
- `make build` succeeds

title: Create auth provider config and shared SignInButtons component
description:
## Summary
Create a centralized auth provider config and a shared `<SignInButtons />` component for rendering social sign-in buttons. These are reused across the sign-in page, student registration, and invitation acceptance. Providers: Google, GitHub, and Microsoft.

## Files to create
- `frontend/src/config/auth-providers.ts` — provider configuration (static data only, no Firebase imports)
- `frontend/src/components/ui/SignInButtons.tsx` — shared sign-in buttons component

## Implementation steps

### 1. Create provider config (`frontend/src/config/auth-providers.ts`)
This file contains only static data — NO Firebase imports. Provider instantiation is handled lazily in SignInButtons.
```typescript
export interface ProviderConfig {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  providerType: 'google' | 'github' | 'microsoft';
}

export const authProviders: ProviderConfig[] = [
  { id: 'google.com', name: 'Google', providerType: 'google' },
  { id: 'github.com', name: 'GitHub', providerType: 'github' },
  { id: 'microsoft.com', name: 'Microsoft', providerType: 'microsoft' },
];
```
Adding/removing providers is a one-line config change.

### 2. Create SignInButtons component (`frontend/src/components/ui/SignInButtons.tsx`)
Props interface:
```typescript
interface SignInButtonsProps {
  onSuccess: () => void;
  onError: (error: Error) => void;
  label?: string; // Optional heading like "Sign in to join CS101"
}
```

**Lazy Firebase imports** — following existing patterns in `AuthContext.tsx` and `auth-provider.ts`:
```typescript
async function signInWithProvider(providerType: string) {
  const { signInWithPopup, GoogleAuthProvider, GithubAuthProvider, OAuthProvider } = await import('firebase/auth');
  const { firebaseAuth } = await import('@/lib/firebase');
  
  let provider;
  switch (providerType) {
    case 'google': provider = new GoogleAuthProvider(); break;
    case 'github': provider = new GithubAuthProvider(); break;
    case 'microsoft': provider = new OAuthProvider('microsoft.com'); break;
  }
  return signInWithPopup(firebaseAuth, provider);
}
```

**Test mode strategy** — using `isTestMode()` from `@/lib/auth-provider`:
- In test mode, render a simplified "Test Sign In" button that:
  1. Prompts for email (or uses a default test email)
  2. Sets the test user in localStorage (matching existing TestAuthProvider pattern: `{ externalId, email }`)
  3. Calls `onSuccess`
- This replaces the current test mode sign-in that uses `signIn(email, password)` from AuthContext

Behavior:
- Renders one button per provider from `authProviders` config
- Each button calls `signInWithProvider(provider.providerType)` on click
- On success: calls `onSuccess` prop
- On error: handles popup errors gracefully:
  - `auth/popup-closed-by-user` — silently ignored (user cancelled)
  - `auth/popup-blocked` — show "Please allow popups" message
  - `auth/cancelled-popup-request` — silently ignored (new popup replaced old)
  - Other errors → call `onError` prop
- Loading state: shows spinner on the clicked button while popup is open

### 3. Styling
Follow existing Button component patterns (`frontend/src/components/ui/Button.tsx`):
- Use `variant="outline"` style for provider buttons
- Full width, stacked vertically with gap
- Provider name as button text (e.g., "Continue with Google")

### 4. Tests (`frontend/src/components/ui/__tests__/SignInButtons.test.tsx`)
- Renders one button per provider in production mode
- Renders test sign-in button in test mode

## Existing patterns to follow

title: Update AuthContext to remove signIn and use social provider bootstrap flow
description:
## Summary
Update AuthContext to remove the `signIn(email, password)` method (sign-in now handled by `<SignInButtons />` component) and update the bootstrap flow to always try POST /auth/bootstrap on 404 instead of checking for custom claims.

## Files to modify
- `frontend/src/contexts/AuthContext.tsx`

## Current behavior (lines ~135-175)
- `signIn(email, password)` calls `signInWithEmailAndPassword(firebaseAuth, email, password)`
- On 404 from `getCurrentUser()`, checks for `role=system-admin` custom claim in JWT
- If custom claim present: calls `bootstrapUser()` → POST /auth/bootstrap
- Otherwise: user state stays null

## New behavior
- Remove `signIn` from `AuthContextType` interface and `FirebaseAuthProvider`
- Sign-in is handled by `<SignInButtons />` calling `signInWithPopup` directly, which triggers `onAuthStateChanged` → `loadUser`
- `onAuthStateChanged` listener remains (it fires when `signInWithPopup` succeeds)
- On 404 from `getCurrentUser()`: always try `POST /auth/bootstrap` (no custom claim check)
- If bootstrap returns 403/error: set user to null (triggers "no access" state in UI)
- If bootstrap succeeds: user is loaded normally
- Keep `signOut` unchanged

## Implementation steps
1. Remove `signIn` from `AuthContextType` interface
2. Remove `signIn` method from `FirebaseAuthProvider` class
3. Remove `signInWithEmailAndPassword` import from `firebase/auth`
4. Update bootstrap logic in `loadUser` / `onAuthStateChanged`:
   - Remove custom claims check (`firebaseUser.getIdTokenResult()` → check for role claim)
   - On 404 from `getCurrentUser()`: directly call `bootstrapUser()`
   - If bootstrap 403: set `user` to null, set `loading` to false
5. Update `AuthContextValue` to not include `signIn`
6. Update all consumers of `signIn` from context — the sign-in page is the main one (updated in a separate task)
7. Update `TestAuthProvider` similarly: remove `signIn` method

## Tests
- Update AuthContext tests: remove signIn assertions
- Test bootstrap flow: on 404, tries bootstrap → on 403, user is null
- Test bootstrap flow: on 404, tries bootstrap → on success, user is loaded

### Verification
- `make test-frontend && make typecheck-frontend` passes

title: Replace email/password forms with SignInButtons on all public auth pages
description:
## Summary
Replace email/password forms with social `<SignInButtons />` on auth pages. Create a separate email/password sign-in page at `/auth/signin/email` for testing (no staging env). Add "already signed in" detection to registration pages.

## Files to create
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/auth/signin/email/page.tsx` — email/password sign-in (testing fallback)

## Files to modify
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/auth/signin/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/register/student/page.tsx`
- `frontend/src/app/(public)/invite/accept/page.tsx`

## Implementation steps

### 1. Create email/password sign-in page (`auth/signin/email/page.tsx`)
Separate page for email/password sign-in (testing — no staging environment):
- Simple email + password form using existing `Input` and `Button` components
- Uses `signInWithEmailAndPassword` from Firebase (lazy import)
- Does NOT include account creation — test accounts are created in the Identity Platform console
- On success: redirect to home (AuthContext picks up the Firebase user)
- Link back to `/auth/signin` for social provider sign-in
- In test mode: use existing `TestAuthProvider` flow

### 2. Sign-in page (`signin/page.tsx`)
- Replace email/password form with `<SignInButtons onSuccess={handleSuccess} onError={handleError} />`
- Remove inline email/password state, form fields, validation
- Remove `signIn(email, password)` call (method no longer exists on AuthContext)
- Add subtle link at bottom: "Sign in with email instead" → `/auth/signin/email`
- `handleSuccess`: navigate to dashboard (same as current post-login redirect)
- Add "no access" handling: after sign-in, if user loads as null (GET /auth/me 404 + bootstrap 403), show "You don't have access to this platform" with sign-out button

### 3. Student registration page (`register/student/page.tsx`)
- Keep step 1: enter join code → validate via `getStudentRegistrationInfo(code)` → show class/section info
- Step 2: remove email, password, confirmPassword fields and all password validation
- If already signed in (via `firebaseAuth.currentUser`): skip sign-in, proceed directly to registration
- If not signed in: render `<SignInButtons label="Sign in to join {className}" onSuccess={handleSignIn} onError={handleError} />`
- `handleSignIn`: call `registerStudent(joinCode)` → redirect
- Keep cleanup on backend failure: `firebaseAuth.currentUser?.delete()`

### 4. Invitation acceptance page (`invite/accept/page.tsx`)
- After token validation: show invitation details (email, role)
- Keep optional display name field (already exists on this page)
- Remove password, confirmPassword fields and all password validation
- If already signed in (via `firebaseAuth.currentUser`): skip sign-in, proceed directly to accepting
- If not signed in: render `<SignInButtons label="Sign in to accept invitation" onSuccess={handleSignIn} onError={handleError} />`
- `handleSignIn`: call `acceptInvite(token, displayName)` → redirect based on role
- Keep cleanup on backend failure: `firebaseAuth.currentUser?.delete()`
- Remove `user_already_exists` error type — with social providers, signInWithPopup signs into existing accounts

### 5. Update tests
- Sign-in page tests: mock `<SignInButtons />`, test link to email sign-in page
- Email sign-in page tests: mock `signInWithEmailAndPassword`, test form validation/submit
- Student registration tests: mock `<SignInButtons />`, test "already signed in" path
- Invitation acceptance tests: mock `<SignInButtons />`, test "already signed in" path

### Verification
- `make test-frontend && make typecheck-frontend && make lint-frontend` passes

title: Update invitation email template with branded HTML and social sign-in copy
description:
## Summary Replace the bare-bones 3-line HTML invitation email with a branded template using the indigo palette. Update copy to reflect social sign-in (no password mention). Use dynamic role text since invitations can be for instructors or namespace-admins. ## Files to modify - `go-backend/internal/email/resend.go` (lines 47-52) - `go-backend/internal/email/resend_test.go` ## Current HTML (lines 47-52) ```go HTML: fmt.Sprintf( `

%s has invited you to join %s.

`+ `

Accept Invitation

`+ `

This invitation will expire in 7 days.

`, html.EscapeString(inviterName), html.EscapeString(namespaceName), html.EscapeString(acceptURL), ), ``` ## New HTML template Branded template matching the app's indigo palette: - Indigo gradient header (#4f46e5 → #6366f1) with "Coding Assignment Platform" text - White card on light gray (#f8fafc) background with 12px border-radius, subtle shadow - Heading: "You've been invited to {namespaceName}" (dynamic, same as current subject) - Body: "{inviterName} has invited you to join {namespaceName} on the Coding Assignment Platform." - CTA button: "Get Started" (indigo #4f46e5, 8px border-radius, white text) - Footer: "This invitation expires in 7 days." + "Coding Assignment Platform" - No mention of passwords — user signs in with their preferred provider - 480px max width, system font stack, mobile-responsive ## Implementation steps 1. Replace the inline HTML string in `SendInvitation` with the branded template 2. Keep `html.EscapeString` on all dynamic values (inviterName, namespaceName, acceptURL) 3. Use a Go raw string literal (backtick) for the template to avoid escaping quotes 4. Update the test in `resend_test.go` to assert the new HTML structure ## Notes - Subject line stays as-is: "You've been invited to {namespaceName}" - The acceptURL still points to `/invite/accept?token=` — unchanged - Template doesn't reference specific role (instructor vs namespace-admin) — just "invited to join"

title: Eliminate frontend permission system — use server-provided permissions
description: Frontend maintains 27 fake fine-grained permissions duplicating the backend's 10 coarse permissions. Eliminate the frontend system by returning permissions from the /me API and using those directly.

title: Backend: Return permissions in /me response
description:
Add a permissions array to the GET /auth/me and PUT /auth/me responses so the frontend can use server-provided permissions instead of maintaining its own role-to-permission mapping.

## Files to modify

- `go-backend/internal/handler/auth.go`
- `go-backend/internal/handler/auth_test.go`

## Implementation

1. Add a `meResponse` struct in `auth.go` that embeds `*store.User` and adds `Permissions []string`:
   ```go
   type meResponse struct {
       *store.User
       Permissions []string `json:"permissions"`
   }
   ```

2. In `GetMe`, compute permissions from the **DB user's role** (`auth.Role(user.Role)`), not `authUser.Role`. This keeps the response self-consistent — the returned `role` field and `permissions` array always match. Preview mode will be handled separately by PLAT-gvhg.3 (preview identity swap), which swaps the full profile including permissions on the frontend.
   ```go
   perms := auth.RolePermissions(auth.Role(user.Role))
   permStrings := make([]string, len(perms))
   for i, p := range perms { permStrings[i] = string(p) }
   httputil.WriteJSON(w, http.StatusOK, meResponse{User: user, Permissions: permStrings})
   ```

3. Apply the same wrapper to `UpdateMe`.

4. Update `TestGetMe_Success` to decode into a struct with permissions and verify the array matches `auth.RolePermissions(auth.RoleStudent)`.

## Notes

- Registration endpoints (PostBootstrap, PostAcceptInvite, PostRegisterStudent) do NOT need this — user will call /me on next page load.
- Using `auth.Role(user.Role)` (DB role) rather than `authUser.Role` (context role) ensures the response is self-consistent: `role` and `permissions` always agree. Preview mode inconsistency is deferred to PLAT-gvhg.3.

## Verification

- `make test-api` passes
- `make lint-api` passes

title: Frontend: Replace local permission system with server-provided permissions
description:
Remove the frontend's duplicated role-to-permission mapping (27 permissions) and switch to using the permissions array returned by the backend /me endpoint (10 permissions).

## Files to modify

- `frontend/src/types/api.ts` — Add `permissions?: string[]` to `User` interface
- `frontend/src/hooks/usePermissions.ts` — Rewrite hooks to check `user.permissions?.includes(perm)`
- `frontend/src/lib/permissions.ts` — **Delete** (role mapping replaced by server data)
- `frontend/src/lib/__tests__/permissions.test.ts` — **Delete**
- `frontend/src/hooks/__tests__/usePermissions.test.ts` — Update for new implementation
- `frontend/src/__tests__/contract/auth.integration.test.ts` — Add permissions field assertion

### Callsite updates (5 files)

| File | Current | New |
|------|---------|-----|
| `app/(app)/system/page.tsx` | `hasRolePermission(user.role, 'system.admin')` | `hasPermission(user, 'system.admin')` |
| `app/(app)/system/namespaces/[id]/page.tsx` | `hasRolePermission(currentUser.role, 'system.admin')` | `hasPermission(currentUser, 'system.admin')` |
| `app/(app)/admin/page.tsx` | `hasRolePermission(user.role, 'user.changeRole')` | `hasPermission(user, 'user.changeRole')` |
| `app/(app)/instructor/components/InstructorDashboard.tsx` | `hasRolePermission(user.role, 'class.create')` / `'session.create'` | `hasPermission(user, 'content.manage')` / `'session.manage'` |
| `app/(app)/classes/[id]/page.tsx` | `hasRolePermission(user.role, 'class.read')` | Remove check entirely (vacuous — all roles pass, backend doesn't gate class reads) |

### Additional updates

- `ProtectedRoute.tsx` — already uses hooks from `usePermissions.ts`, no changes needed. The `requiredRole` prop and its role-checking logic (lines 70-78) stays as-is — it's a separate code path used by the public-view page.
- `InstructorDashboard.test.tsx` — Change mock from `jest.mock('@/lib/permissions', ...)` to `jest.mock('@/hooks/usePermissions', ...)` mocking `hasPermission`. The mock user object at line 14 should also get a `permissions` array added for consistency, though the mocked `hasPermission` returning `true` means it won't be checked at runtime.
- `usePermissions.ts`:
  - Change `UserWithRole` type to `{ permissions?: string[]; [key: string]: unknown }`
  - All hooks check `user.permissions?.includes(perm) ?? false`
  - Update `UserRole` import to come from `@/types/api` directly (currently imported from `@/lib/permissions` which re-exports it — that file is being deleted)

## Permission name mapping (frontend → backend)

- `session.create` → `session.manage`
- `class.create` → `content.manage`
- `class.read` → remove (vacuous)
- `session.join` → `session.join` (same)
- `system.admin` → `system.admin` (same)
- `user.changeRole` → `user.changeRole` (same)

## Verification

- `make test-frontend` passes
- `make lint-frontend` passes
- `make typecheck-frontend` passes
- `make check-api-imports` passes
